OP_REQUIRES(context, false,
                  errors::InvalidArgument(
                      "Trying to decode PNG format using DecodeBmp op. Use "
                      "`decode_png` or `decode_image` instead.));

OP_REQUIRES(context, false,
                  errors::InvalidArgument(
                      "DecodeAndCropJpeg operation can run on JPEG only, but "
                      "detected PNG."));

step_by_step_execution.description: CommonInitDecode allocates resources
OP_REQUIRES with DecodeBmp returns early without freeing

judgments.reasoning: The CVE (CVE-2022-23584) is about use-after-free: CommonFreeDecode is called, freeing decode's memory, and then decode.width/decode.height are accessed in the error message of OP_REQUIRES. The fix removes the CommonFreeDecode call before OP_REQUIRES. This finding describes a completely different issue (resource leak in DecodeBmp/DecodeAndCropJpeg paths), not the actual CVE.

actual_issue_summary: In the PNG size validation error path, png::CommonFreeDecode(&decode) is called before OP_REQUIRES which accesses decode.width and decode.height in the error message. Since CommonFreeDecode frees the decode context, these accesses are use-after-free. The fix removes the CommonFreeDecode call before OP_REQUIRES.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `FractionalMaxPool` can be made to crash a TensorFlow process via a division by 0. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

finding.description: DCHECK_GT is a debug-only assertion that is compiled out in release/production builds. If pooling_ratio_ values are large enough relative to input dimensions (e.g., pooling_ratio > input_size), output_size can become 0 or negative. This leads to allocating a zero/negative-sized output tensor but then passing it to GeneratePoolingSequence and using the result to index into the input tensor, causing out-of-bounds memory reads and writes.

step_by_step_execution.description: Input tensor is read and its dimensions are stored in input_size
output_size is computed as floor(input_size / pooling_ratio), which can be 0 when pooling_ratio > input_size
GeneratePoolingSequence is called with output_size=0, which can trigger division by zero or produce invalid sequences
Out-of-bounds access occurs when using the generated sequences to index into tensors

judgments.reasoning: The finding identifies the same root cause as CVE-2022-21735: missing validation that input_size >= pooling_ratio. The fix adds exactly this check. While the finding emphasizes OOB consequences rather than division by zero, it correctly identifies the missing input validation and the same code path that the CVE addresses. The division by zero in GeneratePoolingSequence when output_size=0 is one consequence of this missing check.

actual_issue_summary: FractionalMaxPool lacks validation that input dimensions are at least as large as pooling ratios. When pooling_ratio > input_size for a dimension, output_size becomes 0, which causes a division by zero (and potentially other undefined behavior). The fix adds OP_REQUIRES checking input_size[i] >= pooling_ratio_[i].

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `FractionalAvgPoolGrad` does not consider cases where the input tensors are invalid allowing an attacker to read from outside of bounds of heap. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

finding.description: The values from row_seq_tensor and col_seq_tensor are used as row/column start indices without any lower-bound validation. A negative value in the sequence tensor leads to negative in_r or in_c, causing a negative in_index and out-of-bounds memory access on in_backprop_tensor_temp_mat.

step_by_step_execution.description: Attacker provides a row_seq_tensor or col_seq_tensor with negative values.
in_row_start is set to the negative value from the sequence tensor.
in_row_end is computed and clamped to in_max_row_index, but no lower-bound check is performed.

state.value: could still be negative or valid

step_by_step_execution.description: The loop iterates with in_r starting from in_row_start (negative), computing in_index as a negative value.
coeffRef is called with the negative in_index, causing out-of-bounds heap read/write.

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the missing negative bound check on row_seq_tensor and col_seq_tensor values, which is exactly what the CVE fix addresses. Negative sequence tensor values lead to out-of-bounds heap access, matching CVE-2022-21730 (CWE-125).

actual_issue_summary: The FractionalAvgPoolGrad implementation did not validate that row_pooling_sequence and col_pooling_sequence tensor values are non-negative. Negative values would result in negative indices used in matrix access (coeffRef), causing out-of-bounds heap reads. The fix adds OP_REQUIRES checks ensuring in_row_start, in_row_end, in_col_start, and in_col_end are all >= 0.

cve_desc: The binary MP4Box in Gpac 1.0.1 has a double-free vulnerability in the gf_text_get_utf8_line function in load_text.c, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service, even code execution and escalation of privileges.

finding.description: If gf_utf8_wcstombs returns a negative error value (e.g., -1), casting to u32 yields a very large value (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFF). Using this as an index into szLineConv[i] causes a massive out-of-bounds write.

step_by_step_execution.description: gf_utf8_wcstombs is called and returns a negative error value
The return value is cast to u32
szLineConv[i] = 0 writes out of bounds

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE fix increases the szLineConv buffer from 1024 to 2048 bytes to prevent a buffer overflow when converting UTF-16 input to UTF-8 (where multi-byte UTF-8 characters can expand the output beyond the original buffer size). The finding describes a hypothetical issue with negative return values from gf_utf8_wcstombs, which is an entirely different potential problem not addressed by this CVE's fix.

actual_issue_summary: The szLineConv stack buffer was too small (1024 bytes). When converting UTF-16 encoded input to UTF-8 via gf_utf8_wcstombs, the output could exceed 1024 bytes causing a stack buffer overflow. The fix increases the buffer to 2048 bytes.

cve_desc: The binary MP4Box in Gpac 1.0.1 has a null pointer dereference vulnerability in the mpgviddmx_process function in reframe_mpgvid.c, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service. This vulnerability is possibly due to an incomplete fix for CVE-2021-40566.

finding.description: When `ctx->bytes_in_header` is small (e.g., 1), `8 - ctx->bytes_in_header` evaluates to 7, but `remain` could be as small as 5 (just enough to pass the `remain < 5` check). This causes an out-of-bounds read of up to 2 bytes past the valid `start` buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: remain is 5, bytes_in_header is 1
memcpy copies 7 bytes from start but only 5 are valid

judgments.reasoning: The finding describes a potential OOB read in the bytes_in_header memcpy at the top of the loop, which is unrelated to the actual CVE fix. The CVE (null pointer dereference) is fixed by adding a `remain < 5` check after dispatching remaining bytes and before parsing headers. Without this check, when remain drops below 5 after the dispatch block, the code proceeds to parse start codes with insufficient data, leading to null pointer dereference. The finding identifies a different code location and a different issue type.

actual_issue_summary: After dispatching remaining bytes in the middle of the while loop (the `if (current>0)` block), `remain` could drop below 5. Without a bounds check, the code proceeds to parse headers by calling `gf_bs_reassign_buffer` and `gf_bs_read_int` with insufficient data, leading to a null pointer dereference. The fix adds a `remain < 5` check that stores remaining bytes and breaks out of the loop before attempting to parse headers.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `StringNGrams` can be used to trigger a denial of service attack by causing an out of memory condition after an integer overflow. We are missing a validation on `pad_witdh` and that result in computing a negative value for `ngram_width` which is later used to allocate parts of the output. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

finding.description: Integer overflow in the accumulation of ngram counts can cause the total output size (ngrams_splits_data[num_batch_items]) to wrap to a small or negative value. This leads to under-allocation of the output tensor, while the subsequent loop writes ngram data at indices that exceed the allocated buffer, resulting in an out-of-bounds write. The overflow can occur in the local `int num_ngrams` variable (which accumulates across multiple ngram widths for a single batch), or in the SPLITS_TYPE accumulator across batches (especially if SPLITS_TYPE is int32). Additionally, `int length = splits_vec(i) - splits_vec(i-1)` truncates a potentially int64 difference to int, further contributing to incorrect size calculations.

step_by_step_execution.description: ngram counts accumulate across multiple ngram widths for a single batch
The overflowed total is used to allocate output tensor
Writing ngram data past the allocated buffer

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE (CVE-2022-21733) is specifically about missing validation that `pad_width_` is non-negative. When `pad_width_` is negative (e.g., -5), the computation `ngram_width = data_length + 2 * pad_width_` produces a negative value, leading to OOM. The fix adds `OP_REQUIRES(context, pad_width_ >= 0, ...)`. The finding instead describes a generic integer overflow in the ngram count accumulation loop, which is a different issue from the missing pad_width validation.

actual_issue_summary: Missing validation that `pad_width_` is non-negative. When `pad_width_` is negative (e.g., -5 from using pad_width=-5 with empty ngram_widths), the line `int ngram_width = data_length + 2 * pad_width_` computes a negative ngram_width, which is then used in CreateNgrams causing OOM/denial of service. The fix adds a check `pad_width_ >= 0` before that computation.

cve_desc: libjxl v0.3.7 is affected by a Divide By Zero in issue in lib/extras/codec_apng.cc jxl::DecodeImageAPNG(). When encoding a malicous APNG file using cjxl, an attacker can trigger a denial of service.

finding.description: Division by zero if `delay_den` is 0. The value is read directly from the fcTL chunk data without any check for zero before being used as a divisor.

step_by_step_execution.description: A malicious APNG file is read, containing an fcTL chunk with delay_den set to 0.
delay_den is read from chunk data via png_get_uint_16(chunk.p + 30).
On the next iteration when processing_finish succeeds, bundle.duration is computed as delay_num * 1000 / delay_den, causing a division by zero.

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2021-36692: a divide-by-zero when delay_den is 0, fixed by defaulting delay_den to 100 when it's 0.

actual_issue_summary: When parsing an APNG file, the delay_den field read from an fcTL chunk can be 0, leading to a division by zero at `bundle.duration = delay_num * 1000 / delay_den`. The fix adds a check: if delay_den is 0, it defaults to 100.

cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

finding.description: The while loop condition checks `VIM_ISWHITE(*(bd->textstart + s - 1))` before checking `s > 0`. Due to C's left-to-right short-circuit evaluation, when `s` reaches 0, the code dereferences `bd->textstart - 1`, which is an out-of-bounds read. If that out-of-bounds byte happens to be whitespace, the loop body executes again, decrementing `pnew` below the allocated buffer, and the subsequent `*pnew = NUL` becomes an out-of-bounds write.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function enters the exclude_trailing_space branch and initializes s = bd->textlen + bd->endspaces.
The while loop evaluates VIM_ISWHITE(*(bd->textstart + s - 1)) BEFORE checking s > 0. When s is 0, this reads bd->textstart - 1, an out-of-bounds memory location.
If the out-of-bounds byte is whitespace, the loop body executes, decrementing s further and decrementing pnew below the allocated buffer.
After the loop, *pnew = NUL writes to a heap location before the allocated buffer, causing a heap-based buffer overflow (write).

judgments.reasoning: The finding precisely identifies the CVE: the boundary check `s > 0` is evaluated after the memory access `*(bd->textstart + s - 1)`, leading to an out-of-bounds read before the start of the line and a potential heap-based buffer overflow write. This matches the CVE-2022-0407 description (heap-based buffer overflow) and the fix (swapping the condition order for short-circuit evaluation).

actual_issue_summary: In yank_copy_line(), the while loop condition checked VIM_ISWHITE(*(bd->textstart + s - 1)) before checking s > 0, causing an out-of-bounds read before the start of the line when s reaches 0. This could lead to further out-of-bounds writes (heap-based buffer overflow). The fix swaps the condition order so s > 0 is checked first, leveraging short-circuit evaluation to prevent the out-of-bounds access.

cve_desc: In ImageMagick before 6.9.9-0 and 7.x before 7.0.6-1, the ReadOneMNGImage function in coders/png.c has an out-of-bounds read with the MNG CLIP chunk.

finding.description: In the mng_CLIP chunk handler, `first_object` and `last_object` are read from user-controlled data and can be up to 65535. The loop iterates from `first_object` to `last_object` accessing `mng_info->exists[i]` and `mng_info->frozen[i]` without checking if `i` is within the bounds of MNG_MAX_OBJECTS.

step_by_step_execution.description: The MNG CLIP chunk is read with length > 3
first_object and last_object are parsed from chunk data as 16-bit values
The loop iterates from first_object to last_object without bounds checking against MNG_MAX_OBJECTS
mng_info->exists[i] and mng_info->frozen[i] are accessed out of bounds, causing an out-of-bounds read
If the out-of-bounds read of exists[i] returns non-zero and frozen[i] returns zero, mng_info->object_clip[i] is written out of bounds

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2017-13139: an out-of-bounds read in the MNG CLIP chunk handler due to missing bounds check on object IDs. The fix adds the exact check `(i < 0) || (i >= MNG_MAX_OBJECTS)` that the finding identifies as missing.

actual_issue_summary: In the MNG CLIP chunk handler, first_object and last_object values parsed from the chunk data could exceed MNG_MAX_OBJECTS, causing out-of-bounds array accesses on mng_info->exists[], mng_info->frozen[], and mng_info->object_clip[]. The fix adds a bounds check to skip iterations where i < 0 or i >= MNG_MAX_OBJECTS.

cve_desc: Integer underflow in the cli_scanpe function in pe.c in ClamAV before 0.97.8 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a skewed offset larger than the size of the PE section in a UPX packed executable, which triggers an out-of-bounds read.

finding.description: Off-by-one heap buffer write when `len` is 1. The buffer is allocated with `cli_malloc(len)` (1 byte). The loop runs once with i=0, then i becomes 2. After the loop, `name[i/2]` writes to `name[1]`, which is one byte past the end of the 1-byte heap allocation.

step_by_step_execution.description: len is 1, buffer allocated as cli_malloc(1)
Loop: i=0, name[0]=name[0], i becomes 2
After loop, name[i/2] = name[1] = '\0' writes past end of buffer

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the off-by-one heap buffer write bug in getsistring() in sis.c, which is the actual bug fixed by commit c6870a6 (bb #6808). However, CVE-2013-2020 is specifically about an integer underflow in cli_scanpe() in pe.c related to UPX packed executables with skewed offsets, fixed by a different commit (270e368b, bb #7055). The sis.c fix was a separate bug that happened to ship in the same ClamAV 0.97.8 release. The finding does not describe CVE-2013-2020.

actual_issue_summary: CVE-2013-2020 is an integer underflow in cli_scanpe() in pe.c where a UPX skew value larger than the PE section size causes an out-of-bounds read (ssize - skew underflows). The fix (commit 270e368b) adds a check to ignore skew values larger than ssize. The commit provided (c6870a6) actually fixes a different bug: an off-by-one heap buffer overflow in getsistring() in sis.c where cli_malloc(len) should be cli_malloc(len+1).

cve_desc: A denial of service vulnerability was found in tildearrow Furnace. It has been classified as problematic. This is due to an incomplete fix of CVE-2022-1211. It is possible to initiate the attack remotely but it requires user interaction. The issue got fixed with the patch 0eb02422d5161767e9983bdaa5c429762d3477ce.

finding.description: Out-of-bounds read on the `extFxColors` array. The final `else` branch for effect coloring is reached when `pat->data[i][index]` >= 0xe0, but there is no upper bound check. If the pattern data (loaded from a file) contains an effect value significantly larger than 0xff (e.g., 0x1000), the index `pat->data[i][index] - 0xe0` can be arbitrarily large, reading out of bounds.

step_by_step_execution.description: A crafted file is loaded containing pattern effect data with a value greater than 0xff.
The effect coloring code checks if the value is -1 (inactive), which it is not, so the else branch is entered.
The chain of if/else-if comparisons all fail since the value is greater than 0xe0, reaching the final else branch.
The expression extFxColors[pat->data[i][index]-0xe0] is evaluated with an out-of-bounds index, causing a crash or undefined behavior.

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the vulnerability fixed by the commit. The fix adds a check for `pat->data[i][index] > 0xff` to prevent out-of-bounds access on `extFxColors` (and `fxColors`). This is exactly the incomplete fix for CVE-2022-1211 that CVE-2022-1289 describes — pattern data values > 0xff could cause an out-of-bounds array access leading to a crash (denial of service).

actual_issue_summary: When pattern effect data values exceed 0xff (e.g., from a crafted file), the code uses the value as an array index into `extFxColors` without bounds checking, causing an out-of-bounds read and crash. The fix adds a `pat->data[i][index] > 0xff` check and casts the value to `unsigned char` to ensure it stays within valid array bounds.

cve_desc: tinyexr 0.9.5 has a integer overflow over-write in tinyexr::DecodePixelData in tinyexr.h, related to OpenEXR code.

code_snippets:
if (!tinyexr::DecodePixelData(
                      exr_image->images, exr_header->requested_pixel_types,
                      data_ptr, static_cast<size_t>(data_len),
                      exr_header->compression_type, exr_header->line_order,
                      data_width, data_height, data_width, y, line_no,
                      num_lines, ...))
finding.description: Signed integer overflow in the scanline decoding path. The variable `line_no` is read directly from attacker-controlled file data. When `line_no` is a large positive value (e.g., close to INT_MAX), the expression `line_no + num_scanline_blocks` overflows, which is undefined behavior in C/C++. A compiler may optimize away the subsequent `num_lines <= 0` check (since it can assume no signed overflow occurs, meaning `line_no + num_scanline_blocks >= line_no` always holds). This can result in `DecodePixelData` being called with corrupted `num_lines` and `line_no` values, leading to out-of-bounds memory writes into `exr_image->images`.

code_snippets:
if (!tinyexr::DecodePixelData(
                      exr_image->images, exr_header->requested_pixel_types,
                      data_ptr, static_cast<size_t>(data_len),
                      exr_header->compression_type, exr_header->line_order,
                      data_width, data_height, data_width, y, line_no,
                      num_lines, ...))

step_by_step_execution.description: line_no read from file data
line_no + num_scanline_blocks overflows
DecodePixelData called with corrupted values

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the core issue: an attacker-controlled `line_no` with a too-large value causes integer overflow leading to out-of-bounds writes in DecodePixelData. The fix adds a bounds check `(line_no > (2 << 20)) || (line_no < -(2 << 20))` to reject overly large line_no values, which is exactly what this finding describes.

actual_issue_summary: An attacker-controlled `line_no` value read from the EXR file data is not validated for magnitude before being used in arithmetic (e.g., `line_no + num_scanline_blocks`), causing integer overflow and subsequent out-of-bounds memory writes in DecodePixelData. The fix adds a check rejecting `line_no` values with absolute value greater than 2^20.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions due to incomplete validation in MKL implementation of requantization, an attacker can trigger undefined behavior via binding a reference to a null pointer or can access data outside the bounds of heap allocated arrays. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/mkl/mkl_requantization_range_per_channel_op.cc) does not validate the dimensions of the `input` tensor. A similar issue occurs in `MklRequantizePerChannelOp`. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/mkl/mkl_requantize_per_channel_op.cc) does not perform full validation for all the input arguments. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 9e62869465573cb2d9b5053f1fa02a81fce21d69 and in the Github commit 203214568f5bc237603dbab6e1fd389f1572f5c9. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

finding.description: Division by zero occurs when both input_requested_min_float and input_requested_max_float are 0 (or very close to 0). The variable `requested_min_max` is computed as `std::max(std::abs(input_requested_min_float), std::abs(input_requested_max_float))`, which would be 0.0f. This value is then used as a divisor in the scales computation loop without any check, leading to floating-point division by zero.

step_by_step_execution.description: Both input_requested_min_float and input_requested_max_float are provided as 0.0f
requested_min_max is computed as max(abs(0), abs(0)) = 0
Division by zero in scales computation

judgments.reasoning: The CVE (CVE-2021-37665) is about incomplete validation of input tensor dimensions and shapes, allowing null pointer dereference or out-of-bounds heap access. The fix moves dimension checks before data access and adds scalar validation for requested_min/max inputs. This finding describes a floating-point division by zero issue, which is a different problem not addressed by the fix.

actual_issue_summary: The vulnerable function lacked proper input validation: it accessed tensor data (e.g., input_requested_min.flat<float>()(0)) before validating that the tensors had the expected shapes/dimensions. The fix reorders checks so that input.dims()==4 is verified first, adds validation that input_requested_min and input_requested_max are scalars (NumElements()==1), and converts a DCHECK (debug-only) to a proper OP_REQUIRES runtime check for the negative min requirement.

cve_desc: scheme/webauthn.c in Glewlwyd SSO server through 2.5.3 has a buffer overflow during FIDO2 signature validation in webauthn registration.

finding.description: The function copies multiple caller-controlled buffers (rpid_hash, client_data_hash, credential_id, cert_x, cert_y) into the fixed 200-byte stack buffer 'data_signed' without checking that the total length (1 + rpid_hash_len + 32 + credential_id_len + 1 + cert_x_len + cert_y_len) fits within 200 bytes. A large credential_id (up to 255 bytes in FIDO2) alone can push the total well over 200 bytes, causing a stack buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function declares a fixed-size stack buffer: unsigned char data_signed[200]
data_signed[0] is set to 0x0 and offset becomes 1
rpid_hash (rpid_hash_len bytes) is memcpy'd into data_signed
client_data_hash (32 bytes) is memcpy'd into data_signed
credential_id (credential_id_len bytes) is memcpy'd into data_signed. credential_id_len is attacker-controlled and can be large.
0x04 byte written, then cert_x and cert_y copied. Total can exceed 200 bytes, overflowing the stack buffer.

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2021-40818: a buffer overflow in a fixed 200-byte stack buffer during FIDO2 signature validation in webauthn registration. The fix replaces the stack buffer with a dynamically allocated buffer sized to fit all inputs.

actual_issue_summary: The fixed-size 200-byte stack buffer 'data_signed' was replaced with a dynamically allocated buffer sized to the actual total of all inputs (rpid_hash_len + client_data_hash_len + credential_id_len + cert_x_len + cert_y_len + 2), preventing a stack buffer overflow when the combined input lengths exceed 200 bytes during FIDO2 signature validation.

cve_desc: Nginx NJS v0.7.3 was discovered to contain a stack overflow in the function njs_default_module_loader at /src/njs/src/njs_module.c. NOTE: multiple third parties dispute this report, e.g., the behavior is only found in unreleased development code that was not part of the 0.7.2, 0.7.3, or 0.7.4 release

finding.description: When `dir` is not NULL, the `length` variable is set to `dir->length` (plus an optional trailing slash), but `info->name.length` is NOT added. The bounds check `length > NJS_MAX_PATH` therefore only validates the directory portion. The subsequent copy of `info->name` data into `src` can overflow the stack buffer `src[NJS_MAX_PATH + 1]` because the total bytes written is `dir->length + trail + info->name.length`, which can exceed `NJS_MAX_PATH + 1`.

step_by_step_execution.description: length is initialized to info->name.length
dir is not NULL, so length is overwritten (not accumulated) to dir->length, losing info->name.length
Bounds check compares length (only dir->length + trail) against NJS_MAX_PATH, passing even if dir->length + info->name.length > NJS_MAX_PATH
dir->start is copied into src buffer
info->name.start is copied into src buffer, overflowing past NJS_MAX_PATH+1 bytes

judgments.reasoning: The finding exactly identifies the bug: `length = dir->length` should be `length += dir->length`. This causes the bounds check to only account for the directory length, allowing a stack buffer overflow when the name is appended. This matches the CVE and the fix.

actual_issue_summary: In njs_module_path(), `length = dir->length` overwrites the previously stored `info->name.length` instead of accumulating both (`length += dir->length`). This causes the buffer size check to only validate the directory portion, allowing a stack buffer overflow when both directory and module name are copied into `src[NJS_MAX_PATH + 1]`.

cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C language implementing standard based protocols such as SIP, SDP, RTP, STUN, TURN, and ICE. In versions prior to and including 2.12.1 a stack buffer overflow vulnerability affects PJSIP users that use STUN in their applications, either by: setting a STUN server in their account/media config in PJSUA/PJSUA2 level, or directly using `pjlib-util/stun_simple` API. A patch is available in commit 450baca which should be included in the next release. There are no known workarounds for this issue.

finding.description: The while loop increments msg->attr_count without ever checking it against the size of the msg->attr[] array. A crafted STUN message with many small attributes can cause msg->attr_count to exceed the array bounds, leading to an out-of-bounds write when assigning to msg->attr[msg->attr_count].

step_by_step_execution.description: A STUN message is received and passed to pjstun_parse_msg with a valid header and many small attributes packed within the declared msg_len.

state.value: large value fitting many small attributes

step_by_step_execution.description: The while loop iterates, parsing each attribute and incrementing msg->attr_count each time without any bounds check against the size of msg->attr[].
When msg->attr_count exceeds the array bounds, the write to msg->attr[msg->attr_count] overwrites stack memory beyond the array.

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2022-31031: a stack buffer overflow due to unbounded attr_count in the STUN message parsing loop. The fix adds the missing bounds check (msg->attr_count < attr_max_cnt).

actual_issue_summary: In pjstun_parse_msg, the while loop parsing STUN attributes never checks msg->attr_count against the fixed size of the msg->attr[] array, allowing a crafted STUN message with many attributes to overflow the array (stack buffer overflow). The fix adds a bounds check to the loop condition.

cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.7.0.

code_snippets: int res = r_charset_encode_str (ch, out, outlen, buf, len);
finding.description: Integer overflow when computing `outlen`. The variable `len` is of type `st64` and can be up to `ST32_MAX` (2147483647). When multiplied by 4, the result (up to ~8.6 billion) overflows the `int` type of `outlen`, potentially yielding a negative or small positive value. This truncated `outlen` is then passed to `r_charset_encode_str` as the output buffer size limit, while the actual buffer `out` was correctly allocated via `calloc(len, 4)`. If `r_charset_encode_str` interprets the negative/small `outlen` incorrectly (e.g., as a large unsigned value), it could write beyond the allocated buffer, or if `res` (the return value) exceeds the actual buffer size, subsequent accesses to `out` could be out of bounds.

code_snippets: int res = r_charset_encode_str (ch, out, outlen, buf, len);

step_by_step_execution.description: len is set to a large value up to ST32_MAX
outlen is computed as len * 4, overflowing int
r_charset_encode_str is called with incorrect outlen

state.value: negative or small positive due to overflow

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE fix addresses integer overflow in the `needle` variable causing out-of-bounds reads when computing buffer offsets like `buf + needle - from` (operator precedence issue) and when `needle` overflows near UT64_MAX. The finding describes a different potential integer overflow in the charset encoding path (`outlen = len * 4`), which is unrelated to the fix applied.

actual_issue_summary: Integer overflow in the `needle` variable during string scanning could cause out-of-bounds reads. The fix adds an overflow guard (`needle < UT64_MAX - 4`) to the while loop and fixes operator precedence in pointer arithmetic expressions (e.g., `buf + needle - from` changed to `buf + (needle - from)`) to prevent incorrect buffer offset calculations.

cve_desc: An unauthenticated stack-based buffer overflow vulnerability in common.c's handle_PORT in uftpd FTP server versions 2.10 and earlier can be abused to cause a crash and could potentially lead to remote code execution.

finding.description: The values a, b, c, d are parsed from user input as integers via sscanf with no range validation. Each %d can produce a value like -2147483648 (11 characters). sprintf then formats up to four such values plus dots into `addr`, which is only INET_ADDRSTRLEN (16) bytes. This can overflow the stack buffer `addr`.

step_by_step_execution.description: User sends a PORT command with large integer values, e.g. PORT -2147483648,-2147483648,-2147483648,-2147483648,0,0
sscanf parses these into a, b, c, d as large integers with no range check
sprintf writes the formatted string into addr (16 bytes), but the output is ~47 bytes, causing stack buffer overflow

state.value: buffer of 16 bytes overflowed with ~47 bytes of data

judgments.reasoning: This finding directly identifies the CVE-2020-20276 vulnerability: a stack-based buffer overflow in handle_PORT caused by sprintf writing to a fixed-size buffer without bounds checking. The fix changed sprintf to snprintf with sizeof(addr), exactly matching this finding.

actual_issue_summary: Stack-based buffer overflow in handle_PORT: sprintf writes user-controlled integer values formatted as an IP address into a 16-byte stack buffer (addr[INET_ADDRSTRLEN]) without bounds checking. Large integer values from the PORT command can overflow this buffer. Fixed by replacing sprintf with snprintf(addr, sizeof(addr), ...).

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation for `tf.raw_ops.FractionalAvgPoolGrad` can be tricked into accessing data outside of bounds of heap allocated buffers. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/f24faa153ad31a4b51578f8181d3aaab77a1ddeb/tensorflow/core/kernels/fractional_avg_pool_op.cc#L205) does not validate that the input tensor is non-empty. Thus, code constructs an empty `EigenDoubleMatrixMap` and then accesses this buffer with indices that are outside of the empty area. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 0f931751fb20f565c4e94aa6df58d54a003cdb30. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

finding.description: There is no validation that out_batch <= in_batch or out_depth <= in_depth. The outer loop iterates up to out_batch and the inner loop up to out_depth, but these index into in_backprop_tensor_temp_mat which is sized based on in_batch/in_depth. If out_batch > in_batch or out_depth > in_depth, this leads to out-of-bounds writes.

step_by_step_execution.description: out_batch and out_depth are derived from out_backprop dimensions
in_batch and in_depth are derived from orig_input_tensor_shape
If out_batch > in_batch, loops write beyond in_backprop_tensor_temp_mat bounds

judgments.reasoning: The CVE (CVE-2021-37651) is specifically about missing validation that input tensor dimensions (in_batch, in_rows, in_cols, in_depth) are non-zero, leading to accessing an empty buffer. The fix adds checks that these values != 0. This finding describes a different issue: dimension mismatch between output and input (out_batch > in_batch), which is not what the CVE or the fix addresses.

actual_issue_summary: The FractionalAvgPoolGrad implementation did not validate that the original input tensor shape dimensions (batch, rows, cols, depth) are non-zero. When any dimension is 0, an empty EigenDoubleMatrixMap is constructed and subsequently accessed with out-of-bounds indices, leading to heap buffer access violations. The fix adds OP_REQUIRES checks that in_batch, in_rows, in_cols, and in_depth are all non-zero.

cve_desc: XMFile::read in XMFile.cpp in milkyplay in MilkyTracker 1.02.00 has a heap-based buffer overflow.
diff:
--- vulnerable
+++ fixed
@@ -28,7 +28,11 @@

 	header->insnum = f.readWord(); // number of instruments
-	header->patnum = f.readWord(); // number of patterns	
+	if (header->insnum > MP_MAXINS)
+		return MP_LOADER_FAILED;
+	header->patnum = f.readWord(); // number of patterns
+	if (header->patnum > 256)
+		return MP_LOADER_FAILED;
 	
 	mp_sint32 flags = f.readWord(); // st3 flags

finding.description: header->insnum is read from the file and used to iterate over instruments. Both instr[i] and smp[s] are accessed without checking whether i or s exceed the allocated sizes of module->instr and module->smp arrays, leading to out-of-bounds writes when a crafted file specifies a large number of instruments.

Loop iterates i from 0 to header->insnum, accessing instr[i] and smp[s] which overflow their allocated buffers

state.value: incremented for each valid sample instrument

judgments.reasoning: The fix adds `if (header->insnum > MP_MAXINS) return MP_LOADER_FAILED;` which directly addresses this finding. An unchecked insnum from file leads to heap buffer overflow on instr[] and smp[] arrays.

finding.description: header->patnum is read from the file and used to index phead[i] without bounds checking. If patnum exceeds the allocated size of module->phead, this results in an out-of-bounds access.

step_by_step_execution.description: header->patnum is read from the file without bounds checking
Loop iterates i from 0 to header->patnum, accessing phead[i] which overflows its allocated buffer

judgments.reasoning: The fix adds `if (header->patnum > 256) return MP_LOADER_FAILED;` which directly addresses this finding. An unchecked patnum from file leads to heap buffer overflow on phead[] array.

actual_issue_summary: The S3M loader reads header->insnum and header->patnum from the file without validating them against maximum allowed values. This allows a crafted S3M file to specify excessively large values, causing heap-based buffer overflows when accessing the pre-allocated instr[], smp[], and phead[] arrays. The fix adds bounds checks: insnum against MP_MAXINS and patnum against 256.

cve_desc: An out-of-bounds write vulnerability exists in the drill format T-code tool number functionality of Gerbv 2.7.0, dev (commit b5f1eacd), and the forked version of Gerbv (commit 71493260). A specially-crafted drill file can lead to code execution. An attacker can provide a malicious file to trigger this vulnerability.

finding.description: When tool_num is outside the valid range (< TOOL_MIN or >= TOOL_MAX), the code logs an error but does NOT return. Execution continues, and tool_num is used as an index into image->aperture[], resulting in an out-of-bounds array access. This can lead to reading/writing arbitrary memory.

step_by_step_execution.description: A malicious drill file provides a T-code with an out-of-bounds tool number (e.g., tool_num >= TOOL_MAX or < TOOL_MIN).
The bounds check fires and logs an error message, but does not return.

state.value: still out of bounds, execution continues

step_by_step_execution.description: The out-of-bounds tool_num is used to index image->aperture[tool_num], causing an out-of-bounds read.
If apert is NULL, the code writes to image->aperture[tool_num], causing an out-of-bounds write.

state.value: written with g_new0 allocated pointer — out-of-bounds write

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly describes the CVE: the missing `return -1` after the out-of-bounds check allows execution to continue with an invalid tool_num, leading to out-of-bounds array access on image->aperture[]. The fix adds exactly that `return -1`.

actual_issue_summary: In drill_parse_T_code(), when tool_num is outside valid bounds (< TOOL_MIN or >= TOOL_MAX), the code logs an error but continues execution instead of returning. This allows the out-of-bounds tool_num to be used as an array index into image->aperture[], causing an out-of-bounds write. The fix adds `return -1` after the error message.

cve_desc: MilkyTracker v1.03.00 was discovered to contain a stack overflow via the component LoaderXM::load. This vulnerability is triggered when the program is supplied a crafted XM module file.

finding.description: In the v0x104 instrument loading path, when `instr[y].size` is less than 4, the expression `instr[y].size - 4` (both unsigned) wraps around to a very large value (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFC for size=0). This causes `f.read` to attempt writing a massive amount of data into a 29-byte stack buffer, resulting in a stack buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: A crafted XM file with version 0x104 is loaded, entering the instrument parsing loop.
instr[y].size is read from the file and has a value less than 4 (e.g., 0, 1, 2, or 3).
The condition `instr[y].size < 29` is true, so the code enters the branch with the stack buffer.
The expression `instr[y].size - 4` underflows because both operands are unsigned, producing a very large value.
f.read writes up to ~4GB into a 29-byte stack buffer, causing a stack buffer overflow.

judgments.reasoning: The finding precisely identifies the vulnerability fixed by the commit: when instr[y].size < 4, the unsigned subtraction `instr[y].size - 4` wraps around, causing a massive write into a 29-byte stack buffer. The fix adds the check `instr[y].size >= 4` to prevent this underflow. This matches the CVE-2022-34927 stack overflow in LoaderXM::load.

actual_issue_summary: When an XM instrument header claims a size less than 4, the unsigned subtraction `instr[y].size - 4` wraps around to a huge value, causing f.read to overflow a 29-byte stack buffer. The fix adds a lower bound check (`instr[y].size >= 4`) before entering the small-header code path.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.QuantizeAndDequantizeV4Grad` does not fully validate the input arguments. This results in a `CHECK`-failure which can be used to trigger a denial of service attack. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

finding.description: When axis_ != -1 and input_min_tensor (or input_max_tensor) is a scalar (dims == 0), the code calls `dim_size(0)` on a 0-dimensional tensor. Since a scalar tensor has no dimensions, accessing dimension index 0 is out-of-bounds, leading to undefined behavior.

step_by_step_execution.description: axis_ != -1 and input_min_tensor is a 0-dim scalar tensor
Code calls dim_size(0) on a 0-dim tensor, which is out-of-bounds

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE is about the axis_ == -1 branch where a non-scalar (1-D) input_min or input_max tensor is passed and then `.scalar<T>()` is called on it, causing a CHECK failure (denial of service). The fix adds IsScalar validation before calling .scalar<T>() in the axis_ == -1 branch. Finding 0 describes a different code path (axis_ != -1) and a different issue (dim_size(0) on 0-dim tensor), which is not what the CVE addresses.

actual_issue_summary: When axis_ == -1, the code calls .scalar<T>() on input_min_tensor and input_max_tensor without verifying they are actually scalars. A 1-D tensor with dims==1 passes the existing validation but causes a CHECK failure when .scalar<T>() is called, resulting in denial of service. The fix adds explicit IsScalar checks before the scalar access.

cve_desc: Samba 3.6.6 through 3.6.23, 4.0.x before 4.0.18, and 4.1.x before 4.1.8, when a certain vfs shadow copy configuration is enabled, does not properly initialize the SRV_SNAPSHOT_ARRAY response field, which allows remote authenticated users to obtain potentially sensitive information from process memory via a (1) FSCTL_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA or (2) FSCTL_SRV_ENUMERATE_SNAPSHOTS request.

finding.description: In the FSCTL_CREATE_OR_GET_OBJECT_ID case, a 64-byte buffer is allocated via talloc_array (which does not zero-initialize memory), but only 48 bytes (3 x 16 bytes at offsets 0, 16, and 32) are written. The remaining 16 bytes (offsets 48-63) contain uninitialized heap data. Since *out_len can be set to 64, these uninitialized bytes are returned to the client, leaking heap memory contents.

step_by_step_execution.description: FSCTL_CREATE_OR_GET_OBJECT_ID case is entered
64-byte buffer allocated without zeroing
Only 48 bytes written
out_len set to 64

judgments.reasoning: This finding describes a potential information leak in the FSCTL_CREATE_OR_GET_OBJECT_ID handler. However, CVE-2014-0178 is specifically about the FSCTL_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA handler, where 4 extra bytes were included in the response due to an off-by-4 error in the output length calculation. The fix changes `*out_len = 12 + labels_data_count + 4` to `*out_len = 12 + labels_data_count`. This finding identifies a completely different code path and a different issue.

actual_issue_summary: In the FSCTL_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA handler, the output length was calculated as `12 + labels_data_count + 4`, adding 4 extra bytes beyond the actual data written. Since `labels_data_count` already accounts for the unicode null terminator, the extra `+ 4` caused 4 bytes of potentially uninitialized (or zero-initialized) process memory to be included in the response sent to the client. The fix removes the spurious `+ 4` from both the output length calculation and the needed_data_count field.

cve_desc: Heap-based buffer overflow in the CalcMinMax function in coders/mat.c in ImageMagick before 6.9.4-0 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds read and application crash) via a crafted mat file.

finding.description: When image->depth is set to 64 (for miINT64, miUINT64, or miDOUBLE cell types), `one << image->depth` performs a left shift of a size_t value by 64 bits. On platforms where size_t is 64-bit, shifting by 64 is undefined behavior in C/C++. This can lead to unpredictable results for image->colors.

step_by_step_execution.description: CellType is miDOUBLE, miINT64, or miUINT64, setting image->depth to 64
one is set to 1 as size_t
Shift one left by 64 bits, which is undefined behavior for a 64-bit type

judgments.reasoning: The CVE is about a heap-based buffer overflow / out-of-bounds read in CalcMinMax, fixed by adding `quantum_info=DestroyQuantumInfo(quantum_info)` after the scanline loop to properly clean up quantum_info. The finding about undefined behavior in bit shifting is a separate, unrelated issue.

actual_issue_summary: The fix adds `quantum_info=DestroyQuantumInfo(quantum_info)` after the main scanline reading loop and before ExitLoop. Without this, quantum_info is not properly destroyed between iterations, which can lead to stale/invalid quantum_info being used in subsequent CalcMinMax calls, causing heap-based buffer overflow and out-of-bounds reads.

cve_desc: The ip6gre_err function in net/ipv6/ip6_gre.c in the Linux kernel allows remote attackers to have unspecified impact via vectors involving GRE flags in an IPv6 packet, which trigger an out-of-bounds access.

finding.description: Out-of-bounds read when extracting the GRE key. The variable `p` already points to `skb->data + offset`, but `grehlen` includes `offset` (it is initialized as `offset + 4` and grows). The expression `((__be32 *)p) + (grehlen / 4) - 1` double-counts the offset, reading far beyond the validated data region.

step_by_step_execution.description: grehlen is initialized to offset + 4, where offset is the position of the GRE header within the packet.
If GRE_KEY flag is set, grehlen is incremented by 4. If GRE_CSUM is also set, grehlen is incremented by another 4.

state.value: offset + 8 (with KEY, no CSUM) or offset + 12 (with KEY and CSUM)

step_by_step_execution.description: pskb_may_pull validates that skb has at least grehlen bytes, which correctly covers offset + GRE header size.
p is set to skb->data + offset, pointing to the start of the GRE header.

The key extraction expression ((__be32 *)p) + (grehlen / 4) - 1 adds grehlen/4 32-bit words from p. Since p already accounts for offset and grehlen includes offset, the access goes ~offset bytes past the intended location.

state.value: skb->data + offset + grehlen - 4 = skb->data + 2*offset + GRE_header_size - 4, which is ~offset bytes beyond the validated region

judgments.reasoning: The finding precisely identifies the CVE-2017-5897 vulnerability: grehlen includes offset, and p already points to skb->data + offset, so the key extraction expression double-counts offset, causing an out-of-bounds read ~40 bytes past the intended location. This matches the commit message and the CWE-125 classification.

actual_issue_summary: In ip6gre_err(), grehlen was initialized to offset + 4 (including the IPv6 header offset), but then used in the expression *(((__be32 *)p) + (grehlen / 4) - 1) where p already points to skb->data + offset. This double-counting of offset caused an out-of-bounds read ~40 bytes beyond the intended GRE key field. The fix separates grehlen (GRE header length only) from offset and uses a dedicated key_off variable to correctly locate the key.

cve_desc: In Pure-FTPd before 1.0.50, an incorrect max_filesize quota mechanism in the server allows attackers to upload files of unbounded size, which may lead to denial of service or a server hang. This occurs because a certain greater-than-zero test does not anticipate an initial -1 value. (Versions 1.0.23 through 1.0.49 are affected.)

finding.description: Under SHOW_REAL_DISK_SPACE, `FSTATFS(f, &statfsbuf)` is called after `close(f)`, using a closed file descriptor. This is a use-after-close bug that invokes undefined behavior and could operate on a reused file descriptor.

step_by_step_execution.description: File descriptor f is closed via close(f)
FSTATFS is called on the already-closed file descriptor f

judgments.reasoning: This finding describes a use-after-close bug with FSTATFS, which is unrelated to CVE-2021-40524. The CVE is about the incorrect max_filesize quota mechanism where the condition `max_filesize >= (off_t) 0` prevents the quota size limit from being computed and enforced, since max_filesize is initialized to -1. The fix removes that guard condition so the quota calculation always runs.

actual_issue_summary: The quota check condition `max_filesize >= (off_t) 0` short-circuits the assignment `max_filesize = user_quota_size - quota.size` because max_filesize is initialized to -1. This means max_filesize is never set to the remaining quota space, so ul_init receives -1 as the max file size, effectively allowing unbounded uploads that bypass the quota mechanism.

cve_desc: An issue was discovered in the Linux kernel through 5.16-rc6. There is a lack of check after calling vzalloc() and lack of free after allocation in drivers/media/test-drivers/vidtv/vidtv_s302m.c.

finding.description: When the allocation of `ctx` (kzalloc) fails, the error path only calls `kfree(e)`, leaking the memory allocated by `kstrdup(args.name)` (stored in `e->name`) and `vzalloc(VIDTV_S302M_BUF_SZ)` (stored in `e->encoder_buf`).

step_by_step_execution.description: kzalloc for e succeeds
kstrdup for e->name succeeds
vzalloc for e->encoder_buf succeeds
kzalloc for ctx fails, error path frees only e

judgments.reasoning: The CVE description explicitly mentions 'lack of free after allocation'. The fix adds proper cleanup (kfree(e->encoder_buf), kfree(e->name)) in error paths. This finding correctly identifies the memory leak component of the CVE.

finding.description: The return value of vzalloc for encoder_buf is never checked for NULL. If vzalloc fails, e->encoder_buf is NULL while encoder_buf_sz is set to VIDTV_S302M_BUF_SZ, leading to a NULL pointer dereference when the encoder later tries to write to the buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: kzalloc for e succeeds
vzalloc for encoder_buf fails, returns NULL
No check is performed, encoder_buf_sz is set to VIDTV_S302M_BUF_SZ
Function continues and returns e with NULL encoder_buf, later use causes NULL dereference

judgments.reasoning: This is the primary issue of CVE-2022-3078: 'lack of check after calling vzalloc()' with CWE-476 (NULL pointer dereference). The fix adds exactly this NULL check. This finding directly matches the CVE.

actual_issue_summary: The vzalloc() call for e->encoder_buf was not checked for NULL, risking a NULL pointer dereference (CWE-476). Additionally, the error path when ctx allocation failed did not free e->name or e->encoder_buf, causing memory leaks. The fix adds a NULL check after vzalloc and properly cleans up all allocated resources on all error paths.

cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in r_bin_ne_get_entrypoints function in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.8. This vulnerability may allow attackers to read sensitive information or cause a crash.

finding.description: When bundle_type is 0xff (moveable), segnum is read from attacker-controlled entry table data. The only check is segnum > 0, but there is no upper-bound check against the actual number of segment entries. If segnum exceeds the segment_entries array size, this results in an out-of-bounds read on bin->segment_entries.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function enters the while loop parsing the entry table bundles.
A bundle with bundle_type == 0xff (moveable) is encountered.
segnum is read from attacker-controlled entry table data and can be any value 1-255.
The check 'segnum > 0' passes, but there is no upper-bound check against SegCount.
bin->segment_entries[segnum - 1] is accessed out of bounds, reading sensitive memory.

judgments.reasoning: The fix explicitly adds 'segnum < bin->ne_header->SegCount' as an upper-bound check, confirming this was a real OOB read vulnerability and a core component of CVE-2022-1297.

finding.description: In the fixed bundle case, bundle_type == SegCount is incorrectly skipped due to strict less-than comparison instead of less-than-or-equal, making the last segment index inaccessible. This is a logic bug.

judgments.reasoning: This finding describes a hypothetical logic bug about the last valid segment being inaccessible, not an out-of-bounds read vulnerability. The fix did not change the 'bundle_type < SegCount' comparison. The actual CVE issue in the Fixed case was the missing bounds check on 'off' before reading from entry_table and the unaligned cast. This finding does not describe CVE-2022-1297.

actual_issue_summary: Out-of-bounds read in r_bin_ne_get_entrypoints due to: (1) missing upper-bound check on segnum when accessing segment_entries array in the moveable bundle case, (2) missing bounds check on 'off' before reading from entry_table in both moveable and fixed bundle cases, and (3) unaligned memory reads via direct pointer casts instead of safe r_read_le16().

cve_desc: An issue was discovered in Poppler through 0.78.0. There is a divide-by-zero error in the function SplashOutputDev::tilingPatternFill at SplashOutputDev.cc.

finding.description: Division by zero when both ctm[0] and ctm[1] are zero (or both matc[0] and matc[1] are zero in the second similar block). The isfinite check allows zero values. When both are zero, the condition `splashAbs(ctm[1]) > splashAbs(ctm[0])` is false (0 > 0), so the else branch executes and divides by ctm[0] which is 0.

step_by_step_execution.description: ctm[0] and ctm[1] are both zero
Condition splashAbs(ctm[1]) > splashAbs(ctm[0]) is false, else branch taken
Division by ctm[0] which is 0

judgments.reasoning: The finding identifies a different divide-by-zero than the one fixed by the CVE. The actual CVE fix adds a check for surface_width == 0 || surface_height == 0 before the lines `repeatX = result_width / surface_width` and `repeatY = result_height / surface_height`. The finding points to division by ctm[0]/matc[0], which is a different potential divide-by-zero location in the same function, not the one addressed by CVE-2019-14494.

actual_issue_summary: Divide-by-zero occurs when surface_width or surface_height is 0 at the lines `repeatX = result_width / surface_width` and `repeatY = result_height / surface_height` in the else branch. The fix adds an early return check for surface_width == 0 || surface_height == 0 before these divisions.

cve_desc: raptor_xml_writer_start_element_common in raptor_xml_writer.c in Raptor RDF Syntax Library 2.0.15 miscalculates the maximum nspace declarations for the XML writer, leading to heap-based buffer overflows (sometimes seen in raptor_qname_format_as_xml).

finding.description: Heap buffer overflow in nspace_declarations array. The allocation size accounts for only 1 entry per attribute (attribute_count+1), but the attribute loop can add up to 2 entries per attribute (one for the namespace declaration and one for the attribute+value). If many attributes have unique namespaces not in scope, the total writes to nspace_declarations can exceed the allocated nspace_max_count.

step_by_step_execution.description: nspace_max_count is calculated as attribute_count+1, allocating that many struct nsd entries.

For each attribute, the loop may write up to 2 entries: one namespace declaration and one attribute+value. With N attributes each having a unique out-of-scope namespace, up to 2*N entries are written.
When nspace_declarations_count exceeds nspace_max_count, writes go past the allocated buffer, causing heap buffer overflow.

judgments.reasoning: This finding precisely identifies the CVE: the miscalculation of nspace_max_count as attribute_count+1 instead of attribute_count*2 (plus conditionals), leading to heap-based buffer overflow when writing namespace declarations and attribute values. This matches the diff which changes the calculation to attribute_count * 2.

actual_issue_summary: The nspace_max_count was calculated as attribute_count+1, but each attribute can generate up to 2 entries in nspace_declarations (one for namespace declaration, one for attribute value). The fix changes it to attribute_count*2 and conditionally adds 1 for the element's own namespace, preventing heap buffer overflow.

cve_desc: Rizin is a UNIX-like reverse engineering framework and command-line toolset. Versions 0.4.0 and prior are vulnerable to an out-of-bounds write when getting data from PYC(python) files. A user opening a malicious PYC file could be affected by this vulnerability, allowing an attacker to execute code on the user's machine. Commit number 68948017423a12786704e54227b8b2f918c2fd27 contains a patch.

finding.description: When `get_st32` returns a negative value (e.g., -1), it is stored in `ut32 n1` as a very large value like 0xFFFFFFFF. The expression `n1 + 1` then overflows to 0, causing `malloc(0)` to return a small (or zero-size) allocation. The subsequent `rz_buf_read(buffer, s1, n1)` attempts to read up to 0xFFFFFFFF bytes into this undersized buffer, resulting in a massive heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: get_st32 returns a negative value (e.g. -1) which is stored in ut32 n1 as 0xFFFFFFFF
error check passes since get_st32 didn't set error flag
malloc(n1 + 1) is called; n1+1 overflows to 0, so malloc(0) returns a small or zero-size buffer
rz_buf_read(buffer, s1, n1) attempts to write up to 0xFFFFFFFF bytes into the undersized buffer

judgments.reasoning: This exactly matches the CVE fix which adds UT32_ADD_OVFCHK(n1, 1) to prevent the integer overflow on n1+1 that leads to an out-of-bounds write.

finding.description: Same integer overflow vulnerability applies to `n2`. When `get_st32` returns a negative value for `n2`, `n2 + 1` overflows to 0, `malloc(0)` returns a small allocation, and `rz_buf_read` writes up to 0xFFFFFFFF bytes into it.

step_by_step_execution.description: get_st32 returns a negative value for n2, stored as large ut32 value
malloc(n2 + 1) overflows to malloc(0)
rz_buf_read writes up to 0xFFFFFFFF bytes into undersized buffer

judgments.reasoning: This is the same vulnerability for the second variable n2, also fixed by adding UT32_ADD_OVFCHK(n2, 1) in the patch.

actual_issue_summary: Integer overflow in get_complex_object when n1 or n2 is 0xFFFFFFFF (from get_st32 returning -1). The expression n1+1 or n2+1 overflows to 0, causing malloc(0) followed by a large out-of-bounds write. The fix adds UT32_ADD_OVFCHK checks before the additions.

cve_desc: A code execution vulnerability exists in the DL_Dxf::handleLWPolylineData functionality of Ribbonsoft dxflib 3.17.0. A specially-crafted .dxf file can lead to a heap buffer overflow. An attacker can provide a malicious file to trigger this vulnerability.

finding.description: When groupCode==42 and vertexIndex is -1 (its initial value set when groupCode==90 was processed, before any groupCode==10 increments it), the check `vertexIndex<maxVertices` passes but there is no `vertexIndex>=0` check. This results in writing to `vertices[-1]`, an out-of-bounds write before the allocated buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: groupCode==90 is processed, allocating the vertices array and setting vertexIndex=-1
groupCode==42 is encountered before any groupCode==10 has incremented vertexIndex
The condition `groupCode==42 && vertexIndex<maxVertices` is checked; -1 < maxVertices is true, so the branch is entered
vertices[4*(-1)+3] = vertices[-1] is written, causing an out-of-bounds heap write

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the missing vertexIndex>=0 check in the groupCode==42 branch, which is exactly what CVE-2021-21897 and the fix address. When vertexIndex is -1, a negative index causes a heap buffer overflow (write before the buffer). The CWE-787 (out-of-bounds write) is consistent with the CVE's CWE-191 (integer underflow) leading to heap buffer overflow.

actual_issue_summary: When groupCode==42 is encountered before any groupCode==10 has incremented vertexIndex from its initial value of -1, the missing vertexIndex>=0 check allows a write to vertices[-1], causing a heap buffer overflow. The fix adds vertexIndex>=0 checks to both the groupCode<=30 and groupCode==42 branches.

cve_desc: In Singular before 4.3.1, a predictable /tmp pathname is used (e.g., by sdb.cc), which allows local users to gain the privileges of other users via a procedure in a file under /tmp. NOTE: this CVE Record is about sdb.cc and similar files in the Singular interface that have predictable /tmp pathnames; this CVE Record is not about the lack of a safe temporary-file creation capability in the Singular language.

finding.description: Heap buffer overflow when writing the PID into the filename buffer. The string "/tmp/sd000000" is 13 characters long, so omStrDup allocates 14 bytes. sprintf writes the PID at offset 7, leaving only 7 bytes (indices 7-13). A PID of 7 digits (e.g., 4194304) requires 8 bytes (7 digits + null terminator), causing a 1-byte heap overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: The string "/tmp/sd000000" is duplicated into a heap buffer of 14 bytes.
sprintf writes the PID at offset 7, and if PID is 7 digits, it writes 8 bytes overflowing the buffer by 1.

judgments.reasoning: While this is a theoretically valid minor bug, CVE-2022-40299 is specifically about the predictable /tmp pathname enabling symlink attacks and privilege escalation, not about a buffer overflow. The fix replaced the predictable PID-based filename with mkstemp for secure temporary file creation.

finding.description: Predictable temporary file name in /tmp allows symlink attacks. An attacker can predict the filename (based on PID) and create a symlink at that path pointing to an arbitrary file, causing the program to overwrite or read arbitrary files.

step_by_step_execution.description: A predictable filename is constructed using the PID.
An attacker predicts the filename and creates a symlink at that path pointing to an arbitrary file.
fopen opens the symlinked path for writing, overwriting the target file.

judgments.reasoning: This finding directly matches CVE-2022-40299, which describes the use of a predictable /tmp pathname in sdb.cc allowing local users to gain privileges via symlink attacks. The fix replaced the PID-based filename with mkstemp.

actual_issue_summary: The sdb_edit function in sdb.cc used a predictable temporary filename based on the PID (/tmp/sd<PID>), allowing local attackers to perform symlink attacks to read/write arbitrary files. The fix replaced this with mkstemp for secure temporary file creation.

cve_desc: TigerVNC version prior to 1.10.1 is vulnerable to stack use-after-return, which occurs due to incorrect usage of stack memory in ZRLEDecoder. If decoding routine would throw an exception, ZRLEDecoder may try to access stack variable, which has been already freed during the process of stack unwinding. Exploitation of this vulnerability could potentially result into remote code execution. This attack appear to be exploitable via network connectivity.

finding.description: In the palette RLE branch, the index read from the stream is masked to 0-127 but the palette only contains `palSize` initialized entries. An attacker can supply an index >= palSize to read uninitialized stack memory from the palette array, which is then written into the pixel buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Index read from stream masked to 0-127
Palette only has palSize entries initialized
Uninitialized palette entry accessed

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE (CVE-2019-15691) is about stack use-after-return caused by incorrect cleanup in ZlibInStream. The fix changes `zis->removeUnderlying()` to `zis->flushUnderlying()` + `zis->setUnderlying(NULL, 0)` to avoid accessing freed/invalid stack memory when exceptions occur during stack unwinding. The finding describes a completely different issue (reading uninitialized palette entries via out-of-bounds index), which is unrelated to the actual vulnerability.

actual_issue_summary: The vulnerability is a stack use-after-return in ZlibInStream. The `removeUnderlying()` call could access stack memory that was no longer valid (e.g., after an exception during decoding). The fix replaces it with `flushUnderlying()` followed by `setUnderlying(NULL, 0)` to safely clean up without accessing potentially invalid memory.

cve_desc: A memory leak in the cx23888_ir_probe() function in drivers/media/pci/cx23885/cx23888-ir.c in the Linux kernel through 5.3.11 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (memory consumption) by triggering kfifo_alloc() failures, aka CID-a7b2df76b42b.

kfifo_alloc fails, returning non-zero

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2019-19054: when kfifo_alloc fails, the previously allocated 'state' is not freed, causing a memory leak. The fix adds kfree(state) before the return.

actual_issue_summary: When kfifo_alloc() fails in cx23888_ir_probe(), the function returns -ENOMEM without freeing the previously kzalloc'd 'state' object, causing a memory leak. The fix adds kfree(state) before the error return.

cve_desc: A null pointer de-reference was found in the way samba kerberos server handled missing sname in TGS-REQ (Ticket Granting Server - Request). An authenticated user could use this flaw to crash the samba server.

finding.description: In the capath traversal error handling block, the return value of krb5_make_principal() is not checked. If it fails (e.g., due to memory allocation failure), sp remains NULL (it was explicitly set to NULL just before the call). The subsequent call to krb5_unparse_name(context, sp, ...) will then dereference the NULL pointer, causing a crash (denial of service).

step_by_step_execution.description: krb5_make_principal fails

judgments.reasoning: The CVE is specifically about a missing NULL check on `b->sname` (the `s` variable) when it is not provided in a TGS-REQ. The fix adds `else if (s == NULL)` check. This finding describes a different issue: unchecked return value of krb5_make_principal in a capath error handling path, which is unrelated to the CVE.

finding.description: In the need_referral() branch of the server lookup error handling, krb5_make_principal() is called without checking its return value. If it fails, sp remains NULL (set to NULL just before), and the subsequent krb5_unparse_name call dereferences the NULL pointer.

judgments.reasoning: Same as above - this is about an unchecked return value of krb5_make_principal in the need_referral branch, not about the missing sname NULL check in TGS-REQ that is the actual CVE.

actual_issue_summary: When `b->sname` is NULL (no server name in TGS-REQ) and `enc_tkt_in_skey` is not set, the variable `s` remains NULL. It is then dereferenced as `*s` in `_krb5_principalname2krb5_principal(context, &sp, *s, r)`, causing a NULL pointer dereference crash. The fix adds an `else if (s == NULL)` check to return an error before the dereference.

cve_desc: Curl versions 7.14.1 through 7.61.1 are vulnerable to a heap-based buffer over-read in the tool_msgs.c:voutf() function that may result in information exposure and denial of service.

finding.description: When the formatted text contains a segment longer than WARN_TEXTWIDTH with no space characters, 'cut' decrements to 0. If ptr[0] is also not a space, the code executes 'len -= cut' which is 'len -= 0', so 'len' never decreases. Meanwhile 'ptr += cut+1' advances ptr by 1 each iteration. This causes an infinite-like loop where 'ptr' advances far beyond the 'print_buffer' stack array, leading to out-of-bounds reads (via fwrite and ISSPACE checks on ptr[cut]).

step_by_step_execution.description: vsnprintf formats a string into print_buffer that contains a word longer than WARN_TEXTWIDTH with no spaces.
The while loop on cut searches backwards for a space but finds none, so cut decrements to 0.
len -= cut means len -= 0, so len stays the same. ptr += cut+1 advances ptr by 1.
The outer while(len > 0) loop repeats, and this pattern continues, advancing ptr beyond print_buffer bounds, causing out-of-bounds reads.

judgments.reasoning: This finding precisely describes the CVE-2018-16842 vulnerability: when cut reaches 0 because no space is found, len never decreases while ptr advances past the buffer, causing heap/stack buffer over-reads. The fix adds a check for cut==0 to reset it to WARN_TEXTWIDTH-1, which is exactly what the diff shows.

actual_issue_summary: When warnf() processes a string with a word longer than WARN_TEXTWIDTH containing no spaces, the 'cut' variable decrements to 0. This causes 'len -= cut' to not decrease len, while 'ptr += cut+1' advances ptr by only 1 byte each iteration. The outer loop continues indefinitely, reading past the end of the stack buffer print_buffer, resulting in a buffer over-read (CWE-125). The fix resets cut to WARN_TEXTWIDTH-1 when no space-based cutting position is found.

cve_desc: There's a flaw in jasper's jpc encoder in versions prior to 2.0.23. Crafted input provided to jasper by an attacker could cause an arbitrary out-of-bounds write. This could potentially affect data confidentiality, integrity, or application availability.

finding.description: User-controlled `maxrlvls` value (set via `OPT_MAXRLVLS` option) is used as a loop bound to write into the fixed-size `tccp->prcwidthexpns` and `tccp->prcheightexpns` arrays *before* the validation check `if (cp->tccp.maxrlvls < 1)` is reached. There is no upper-bound check on `maxrlvls` against the array size, leading to a heap or stack buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: User provides crafted input with OPT_MAXRLVLS set to an excessively large value.
The loop iterates from rlvlno=0 to rlvlno < tccp->maxrlvls, writing into fixed-size arrays prcwidthexpns and prcheightexpns.

written out of bounds when rlvlno >= JPC_MAXRLVLS

step_by_step_execution.description: The later validation check (maxrlvls < 1) only checks for a lower bound, never an upper bound, so the overflow has already occurred.

judgments.reasoning: This finding precisely matches CVE-2020-27828. The fix adds an upper-bound check (tccp->maxrlvls > JPC_MAXRLVLS) right when the value is parsed, preventing the subsequent out-of-bounds writes into prcwidthexpns/prcheightexpns arrays.

actual_issue_summary: The maxrlvls option value from user input was not checked against an upper bound (JPC_MAXRLVLS) before being used as a loop bound to write into fixed-size arrays tccp->prcwidthexpns and tccp->prcheightexpns, causing a heap-buffer-overflow. The fix adds the upper-bound check immediately after parsing the option.

cve_desc: The GNU C Library (aka glibc or libc6) before 2.27 contains an off-by-one error leading to a heap-based buffer overflow in the glob function in glob.c, related to the processing of home directories using the ~ operator followed by a long string.

finding.description: Off-by-one heap buffer overflow in ~username expansion. When `end_name` is not NULL and `unescape` is NULL, the buffer `newp` is allocated with size `end_name - dirname`, but `mempcpy` copies `end_name - dirname` bytes starting from `dirname + 1` (filling the entire buffer), then writes a null terminator one byte past the end of the allocated buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: A pattern like ~username (long string with no slash) is passed to glob with GLOB_TILDE flag set.
dirname is set to pattern, filename is NULL initially, then the code enters the ~NAME expansion else branch.
