mempcpy copies end_name - dirname bytes from dirname + 1, which fills the entire buffer, then null terminator is written one byte past the end.

state.value: newp + (end_name - dirname), which is 1 byte past allocated buffer

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2017-15670. The diff changes `end_name - dirname` to `end_name - dirname - 1` in the mempcpy call, fixing the off-by-one error that writes a null byte one past the allocated heap buffer. The finding correctly identifies the root cause, the affected code, and the consequence (heap buffer overflow).

actual_issue_summary: Off-by-one error in glob's ~username expansion: when copying the username from dirname+1 into newp (allocated as end_name - dirname bytes), mempcpy copies end_name - dirname bytes instead of end_name - dirname - 1, causing a one-byte heap buffer overflow when the null terminator is written past the end of the buffer.

cve_desc: A race condition exists in Eternal Terminal prior to version 6.2.0 that allows an authenticated attacker to hijack other users' SSH authorization socket, enabling the attacker to login to other systems as the targeted users. The bug is in UserTerminalRouter::getInfoForId().

finding.description: The pipe path from endpoint.name() is copied into local.sun_path (a fixed-size buffer, typically 108 bytes on Linux) using strcpy without any length check. If pipePath exceeds the size of sun_path, a stack buffer overflow occurs.

step_by_step_execution.description: endpoint.name() returns a string longer than sizeof(local.sun_path)
strcpy copies the entire string into the fixed-size buffer without bounds checking

judgments.reasoning: The CVE-2022-24950 is about a race condition in UserTerminalRouter::getInfoForId() due to missing mutex locks, allowing SSH authorization socket hijacking. The finding identifies a buffer overflow (strcpy→strncpy) which, while fixed in the same commit, is a different bug and not the vulnerability described by this CVE.

actual_issue_summary: CVE-2022-24950 is a race condition in UserTerminalRouter where acceptNewConnection() and getInfoForId() access shared state (idInfoMap) without synchronization. The fix adds a recursive_mutex (routerMutex) with lock_guard in both methods. The strcpy→strncpy change in PipeSocketHandler was a separate hardening fix in the same commit.

cve_desc: cdf_read_property_info in cdf.c in file through 5.37 does not restrict the number of CDF_VECTOR elements, which allows a heap-based buffer overflow (4-byte out-of-bounds write).

finding.description: On 32-bit platforms, the variable `slen` accumulates string lengths via `slen += l >> 1` inside the inner loop for CDF_LENGTH32_STRING/CDF_LENGTH32_WSTRING vector processing. When `l` values from attacker-controlled data are large, `slen` can grow enough that `slen * sizeof(uint32_t)` overflows the 32-bit `size_t`, causing `o4` to wrap around to a small value. Subsequent bounds checks (`o4 + sizeof(uint32_t) > left` and `o4 + l > left`) then pass incorrectly, allowing out-of-bounds reads from `q[o4]` and `CDF_GETUINT32(q, slen)`.

step_by_step_execution.description: slen accumulates large l values
o4 wraps around

judgments.reasoning: The CVE (CVE-2019-18218) is specifically about not restricting the number of CDF_VECTOR elements (nelements), which allows a heap-based buffer overflow (4-byte out-of-bounds write) when cdf_grow_info is called with an excessively large nelements, or when the loop iterates too many times writing to inp[i]. The fix adds a check `nelements > CDF_ELEMENT_LIMIT`. The finding instead describes a hypothetical integer overflow in slen/o4 on 32-bit platforms due to large string lengths, which is a different mechanism (integer overflow in offset computation causing OOB reads) than the actual CVE (unrestricted vector element count causing heap-based OOB write). The root cause and exploitation path described do not match the CVE.

actual_issue_summary: CVE-2019-18218: The nelements value read from CDF_VECTOR was not bounded by an upper limit (only checked for zero). An attacker could craft a file with a very large nelements, causing a heap-based buffer overflow (4-byte out-of-bounds write) when the code processes more vector elements than the allocated buffer can hold. The fix adds a check `nelements > CDF_ELEMENT_LIMIT` to reject excessively large element counts.

cve_desc: A buffer overflow vulnerability was found in the Linux kernel Intel’s iSMT SMBus host controller driver in the way it handled the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL case (via the ioctl I2C_SMBUS) with malicious input data. This flaw could allow a local user to crash the system.

finding.description: In the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL case, data->block[0] is used directly as the memcpy size without any bounds validation. If data->block[0] exceeds the size of the DMA buffer (priv->buffer), this causes an out-of-bounds write.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with size == I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL and data->block[0] set to a value greater than I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_MAX (e.g., 255).
Execution enters the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL case with no bounds check on data->block[0].
memcpy copies data->block[0] bytes from data->block[1] into dma_buffer[1], writing beyond the end of the DMA buffer.

judgments.reasoning: This finding correctly identifies the CVE-2022-3077 vulnerability: the missing bounds check on data->block[0] in the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL case allows a buffer overflow via memcpy. The fix adds exactly this check (data->block[0] > I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_MAX).

actual_issue_summary: In the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL case of ismt_access(), data->block[0] (user-controlled, 0-255) is used without validation as the size for memcpy into dma_buffer, which can overflow the buffer. The fix adds a check that returns -EINVAL if data->block[0] > I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_MAX.

cve_desc: Buffer Over-read in GitHub repository bfabiszewski/libmobi prior to 0.11.

finding.description: After calling mobi_getstring_ordt or mobi_indx_get_label, the returned label_length is not validated against INDX_LABEL_SIZEMAX. If these functions return a label_length larger than INDX_LABEL_SIZEMAX, the strncpy call will read beyond the stack-allocated text buffer, causing a stack buffer over-read. Furthermore, those functions themselves may write beyond the text buffer, causing a stack buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: Entry length and label length pass initial validation checks.
mobi_indx_get_label or mobi_getstring_ordt is called, which may return a label_length exceeding INDX_LABEL_SIZEMAX or write beyond the text buffer boundary.
strncpy reads label_length + 1 bytes from text into the heap-allocated label, reading beyond the stack buffer.

state.value: exceeds text buffer size, causing over-read

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the buffer over-read vulnerability in the same code area as the CVE fix. The fix addresses this by increasing the text buffer size by 1 (INDX_LABEL_SIZEMAX + 1) and adding error checking after mobi_indx_get_label to catch corrupt input that could cause the buffer over-read. The finding's CWE-125 matches the CVE's CWE-125.

actual_issue_summary: The text buffer was sized as INDX_LABEL_SIZEMAX without room for null termination (off-by-one), and there was no error checking after mobi_indx_get_label for corrupt input, allowing a buffer over-read when processing crafted MOBI files with corrupt index entry labels.

cve_desc: A heap buffer overflow issue was found in ImageMagick. When an application processes a malformed TIFF file, it could lead to undefined behavior or a crash causing a denial of service.

finding.description: In the ReadGenericMethod case, the multiplication `image->columns * image->rows` is performed as a `size_t * size_t` operation before being assigned to `MagickSizeType` (64-bit). On 32-bit systems, this wraps around, resulting in a small `number_pixels` value. The HeapOverflowSanityCheck only validates `image->rows * sizeof(*pixels)`, ignoring `image->columns`. The subsequent allocation is too small, and `TIFFReadRGBAImage` writes the full `columns * rows * 4` bytes, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: ReadGenericMethod case is entered
number_pixels computed with potential overflow
TIFFReadRGBAImage writes beyond allocated buffer

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE fix is in the ReadStripMethod case, where the strip_pixels buffer extent calculation was missing the `(image->depth+7)/8` factor, causing an undersized allocation. The finding describes a hypothetical 32-bit overflow in the ReadGenericMethod case, which is a completely different code path and not what CVE-2022-3213 addresses.

actual_issue_summary: In the ReadStripMethod case of ReadTIFFImage, the buffer size for strip_pixels was calculated as `4*(samples_per_pixel+1)*TIFFStripSize(tiff)`, which did not account for image depth. The fix multiplies by `(image->depth+7)/8` to ensure the buffer is large enough, preventing a heap buffer overflow when processing TIFF images with higher bit depths.

cve_desc: In ImageMagick 7.0.8-13 Q16, there is an infinite loop in the ReadBMPImage function of the coders/bmp.c file. Remote attackers could leverage this vulnerability to cause a denial of service via a crafted bmp file.

finding.description: Integer overflow in the computation of `bytes_per_line` and `length`. On 32-bit systems, `image->columns * bmp_info.bits_per_pixel` can overflow, producing a small `bytes_per_line`. Then `bytes_per_line * image->rows` can also overflow to a small `length`. The sanity check `(length/8) > GetBlobSize(image)` is bypassed because `length` is small. The buffer is allocated via `AcquireVirtualMemory` (which does safe overflow-checking multiplication using `image->columns+256UL` as a fallback), so the buffer may be larger than `length`. However, `ReadBlob` only reads the small overflowed `length` bytes, leaving the rest of the buffer uninitialized. The pixel-processing loops then recompute `bytes_per_line` correctly (e.g., `4*(image->columns)` for 32bpp) and access offsets well beyond the initialized portion of the buffer, reading uninitialized heap memory.

step_by_step_execution.description: Integer overflow in bytes_per_line calculation

judgments.reasoning: The CVE is about an infinite loop (CWE-835) caused by `start_position` not being updated on subsequent iterations of the do-while loop when `bmp_info.ba_offset != 0`. The fix removes the conditional guard so `start_position` is always updated. This finding describes an unrelated integer overflow issue, not the infinite loop.

finding.description: Integer overflow in the size parameter passed to DecodeImage for RLE-compressed BMPs. The expression `image->columns * image->rows` can overflow on 32-bit systems when both dimensions are large, passing a much smaller size limit to DecodeImage than the actual buffer size, potentially leading to incorrect bounds enforcement within the RLE decoder.

step_by_step_execution.description: Integer overflow in columns*rows

judgments.reasoning: The CVE is about an infinite loop (CWE-835) caused by `start_position` not being updated correctly across loop iterations. This finding describes an integer overflow in DecodeImage's size parameter, which is unrelated to the actual vulnerability.

actual_issue_summary: The vulnerability is an infinite loop (CWE-835) in ReadBMPImage. The `start_position` variable was only updated when `bmp_info.ba_offset == 0`, meaning on subsequent iterations of the do-while loop (when processing multi-image BMP files with ba_offset), `start_position` retained its old value. This caused seeks to go back to previously-read positions, re-reading the same data and looping forever. The fix removes the conditional so `start_position` is always updated at each iteration.

cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C language implementing standard based protocols such as SIP, SDP, RTP, STUN, TURN, and ICE. In affected versions if the incoming STUN message contains an ERROR-CODE attribute, the header length is not checked before performing a subtraction operation, potentially resulting in an integer underflow scenario. This issue affects all users that use STUN. A malicious actor located within the victim’s network may forge and send a specially crafted UDP (STUN) message that could remotely execute arbitrary code on the victim’s machine. Users are advised to upgrade as soon as possible. There are no known workarounds.

finding.description: If the attribute header length field (read from untrusted network data) is less than 4, the subtraction `attr->hdr.length - 4` results in an integer underflow. If the length type is unsigned, this wraps to a very large value; if signed, it becomes negative but may be implicitly converted to a large unsigned value during the subsequent `pj_strdup` memory allocation and copy, leading to a heap buffer over-read or heap overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: A crafted STUN message with an ERROR-CODE attribute is received where the header length field is less than 4.
The subtraction `attr->hdr.length - 4` produces a negative or wrapped-around value assigned to value.slen.

state.value: negative (if signed) or very large (if unsigned), e.g., -4 or 0xFFFFFFFC

step_by_step_execution.description: pj_strdup is called with this corrupted length, causing an oversized memory allocation and copy from the buffer, leading to heap buffer over-read or overflow.

state.value: negative/wrapped value used as size in memcpy

judgments.reasoning: This finding directly identifies the same integer underflow vulnerability described in CVE-2021-37706: the header length is not checked before the subtraction `attr->hdr.length - 4`, and the fix adds exactly the check `if (value.slen < 0) value.slen = 0` to prevent this.

actual_issue_summary: In decode_errcode_attr(), when parsing a STUN ERROR-CODE attribute, the value `attr->hdr.length - 4` is computed without checking that the header length is at least 4. If it is less than 4, this results in an integer underflow (negative value for slen), which causes pj_strdup to perform an incorrect memory operation. The fix adds a check to clamp value.slen to 0 if it becomes negative.

cve_desc: An issue was discovered in net/tipc/crypto.c in the Linux kernel before 5.14.16. The Transparent Inter-Process Communication (TIPC) functionality allows remote attackers to exploit insufficient validation of user-supplied sizes for the MSG_CRYPTO message type.

finding.description: A heap buffer overflow occurs because `skey->keylen` is read from attacker-controlled message data and used as the length for `memcpy` into the `skey->key` field *before* the sanity check (`size != tipc_aead_key_size(skey)`) validates it. An attacker can send a message where `keylen` is much larger than the allocated buffer (`size` bytes), causing an out-of-bounds write on the heap.

step_by_step_execution.description: User input is read into variable `x`

judgments.reasoning: This finding correctly identifies the core issue of CVE-2021-43267: insufficient validation of user-supplied sizes in MSG_CRYPTO messages. The vulnerable code allocates `size` bytes for `skey` via kmalloc, then reads `skey->keylen` from attacker-controlled data, and uses it as the memcpy length without prior validation. If keylen exceeds the allocated buffer, a heap buffer overflow occurs. The fix adds size validation before the allocation and copy operations.

actual_issue_summary: CVE-2021-43267 is an insufficient validation of user-supplied sizes in TIPC MSG_CRYPTO messages. The function `tipc_crypto_key_rcv` allocates `size` bytes for `skey` but reads `keylen` from attacker-controlled message data and uses it in a `memcpy` before validating that `keylen` is consistent with the allocated size. The fix adds upfront validation that the message size is sufficient and that keylen is consistent with the overall message size and within maximum bounds, before any allocation or copy occurs.

cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository hpjansson/chafa prior to 1.12.0.

finding.description: When code_new equals current_entry and current_entry equals (1 << LZW_CODE_MAX), the code enters the 'code not in table' branch. The dictionary-add is skipped because the condition `current_entry < (1 << LZW_CODE_MAX)` is false, so no entry is written at table[current_entry]. However, the code later reads `table[code_new].first_value` where code_new == current_entry == (1 << LZW_CODE_MAX). If the table is allocated with (1 << LZW_CODE_MAX) entries (indices 0 through (1 << LZW_CODE_MAX) - 1), this is an out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: code_new is read from input and current_entry has grown to (1 << LZW_CODE_MAX).
The check code_new > current_entry is false since they are equal, so execution continues.
code_new < current_entry is false, so the 'code not in table' else branch is taken.
The dictionary-add is skipped because current_entry < (1 << LZW_CODE_MAX) is false.
table[code_new].first_value is accessed where code_new == (1 << LZW_CODE_MAX), which is out of bounds for the table.

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the out-of-bounds read that occurs when code_new == current_entry == (1 << LZW_CODE_MAX). The fix adds exactly the check `code_new >= 1 << LZW_CODE_MAX` to prevent this OOB access, matching the CVE (CWE-125 heap-based buffer over-read in lzw_decode).

actual_issue_summary: In lzw_decode, when code_new equals current_entry and both equal (1 << LZW_CODE_MAX), the table is accessed out of bounds. The fix adds a bounds check `code_new >= 1 << LZW_CODE_MAX` returning LZW_BAD_CODE before the table access.

cve_desc: A denial of service vulnerability was found in rsyslog in the imptcp module. An attacker could send a specially crafted message to the imptcp socket, which would cause rsyslog to crash. Versions before 8.27.0 are vulnerable.

finding.description: The octet count parser accumulates digits into pThis->iOctetsRemain via repeated multiply-by-10-and-add without any upper-bound check. A malicious sender can supply a very long sequence of digits, causing a signed integer overflow. This can make iOctetsRemain wrap to a negative value. When the octet-counting copy path is later entered, `octatesToCopy` takes this negative value. The subsequent guard checks (`buffLen < octatesToCopy` and `octatesToCopy + pThis->iMsg > iMaxLine`) both evaluate false for a negative `octatesToCopy`. The negative int is then implicitly cast to a very large `size_t` in the `memcpy` call, causing a massive heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: Attacker sends a TCP message with octet-counting framing, starting with a very long sequence of digit characters.
The parser enters eInOctetCnt state and accumulates digits into iOctetsRemain without any upper bound check.

state.value: grows with each digit via iOctetsRemain * 10 + c - '0'

step_by_step_execution.description: After enough digits, signed integer overflow occurs, wrapping iOctetsRemain to a negative value.
A space delimiter is received, transitioning to eInMsg state with the overflowed iOctetsRemain.
In the octet-counting branch, octatesToCopy is set to the negative iOctetsRemain. Guard checks fail to catch it because negative comparisons with positive values evaluate false.
memcpy is called with the negative octatesToCopy implicitly cast to a very large size_t, causing a heap buffer overflow and crash.

state.value: very large value due to negative-to-unsigned cast

judgments.reasoning: This finding correctly identifies the integer overflow (CWE-190) in the octet count accumulation without bounds checking, which is exactly what CVE-2018-16881 describes. The fix adds a check (iOctetsRemain <= 200000000) to prevent the overflow. The described crash consequence matches the DoS described in the CVE.

actual_issue_summary: Integer overflow in imptcp's octet count parser: pThis->iOctetsRemain is accumulated without any upper bound check via repeated multiply-by-10-and-add, allowing a crafted message with many digit characters to overflow the signed int. This leads to a segmentation fault / crash (DoS). The fix adds a cap at 200000000 before multiplying, and also handles edge cases for invalid octet counts.

cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.2.

finding.description: Out-of-bounds read when reading `number_of_classes` from the buffer at offset 6 without first checking that `sz` is large enough (at least 8). If `sz` is less than 8, the `R_BIN_JAVA_USHORT(buffer, offset)` macro reads beyond the allocated buffer.

offset is incremented to 6
R_BIN_JAVA_USHORT reads 2 bytes at offset 6, which exceeds the buffer size

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the missing bounds check before reading number_of_classes at offset 6. The fix adds exactly this check: `if (buf_offset + offset + 8 > sz) return NULL;` before that read. While the CVE is classified as CWE-787 (heap buffer overflow), the finding describes a direct component of the same vulnerability — insufficient bounds checking on buffer reads that can lead to out-of-bounds heap access.

actual_issue_summary: Missing bounds check before reading from the buffer at offset 6 (for number_of_classes), and incorrect bounds check inside the loop that omitted buf_offset, allowing out-of-bounds heap access when parsing Java inner classes attributes from a malformed class file.

cve_desc: The qtm_decompress function in libclamav/mspack.c in ClamAV before 0.96 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (memory corruption and application crash) via a crafted CAB archive that uses the Quantum (aka .Q) compression format.  NOTE: some of these details are obtained from third party information.

finding.description: Out-of-bounds write on the window buffer. In both match copy paths, the number of bytes actually copied can be less than match_length (due to clamping in the non-wrapping case, or no clamping in the wrapping case), but window_posn is always advanced by the full match_length. This causes window_posn to exceed window_size, and on the next loop iteration, writes via window[window_posn++] go out of bounds. Additionally, in the wrapping match case (match_offset > window_posn), there is no check that window_posn + match_length <= window_size, so the copy itself can write past the end of the window buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: A crafted CAB archive using Quantum compression is processed by qtm_decompress.
The decompressor reads a selector >= 4 (match), computes match_length and match_offset from the compressed stream.
No check exists to verify that window_posn + match_length <= window_size, so a large match_length can cause the copy to write past the window buffer boundary.
The match copy writes bytes past the end of the window buffer, or window_posn is advanced past window_size causing subsequent literal writes via window[window_posn++] to go out of bounds.

judgments.reasoning: The finding directly identifies the missing bounds check that the fix adds: `window_posn + match_length > qtm->window_size`. This is exactly the vulnerability described by CVE-2010-1311 — a crafted CAB archive with Quantum compression can cause memory corruption via out-of-bounds writes on the window buffer.

actual_issue_summary: The Quantum decompressor in qtm_decompress lacked a check that window_posn + match_length does not exceed window_size before performing match copy operations. A crafted CAB archive could trigger out-of-bounds writes past the window buffer, causing memory corruption and a crash. The fix adds `if (window_posn + match_length > qtm->window_size) return error;` before the copy.

cve_desc: The hfsplus_block_allocate function in fs/hfsplus/bitmap.c in the Linux kernel before 2.6.28-rc1 does not check a certain return value from the read_mapping_page function before calling kmap, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service (system crash) via a crafted hfsplus filesystem image.

finding.description: read_mapping_page() can return an ERR_PTR (error-encoded pointer) on failure (e.g., I/O error, corrupted filesystem). The return value is never checked before being passed to kmap(), which would dereference an invalid pointer, causing a kernel NULL pointer dereference / oops. This can be triggered by a maliciously crafted HFS+ filesystem image.

step_by_step_execution.description: read_mapping_page() is called and returns an ERR_PTR on failure
The error pointer is passed directly to kmap() without checking
kmap() dereferences the invalid pointer, causing a kernel oops/crash

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2008-4934: the return value of read_mapping_page() is not checked with IS_ERR() before being passed to kmap(), allowing a crafted HFS+ filesystem image to trigger a kernel crash. The fix adds IS_ERR() checks at all three call sites.

actual_issue_summary: hfsplus_block_allocate() passes the return value of read_mapping_page() directly to kmap() without checking if it's an error pointer (IS_ERR). A crafted HFS+ filesystem can cause read_mapping_page() to fail, returning an ERR_PTR that when dereferenced by kmap() causes a kernel oops/crash (denial of service). The fix adds IS_ERR() checks after all three read_mapping_page() calls in the function.

cve_desc: MariaDB Server v10.6 and below was discovered to contain an use-after-free in the component my_strcasecmp_8bit, which is exploited via specially crafted SQL statements.

finding.description: If dl->length is smaller than so_ext_len (sizeof(SO_EXT) - 1), the expression `dl->length - so_ext_len` underflows because both operands are of unsigned type `size_t`. This results in a very large positive value, causing `dl->str + dl->length - so_ext_len` to point far out of bounds, leading to an out-of-bounds read in my_strcasecmp.

step_by_step_execution.description: fix_dl_name is called with a dl whose length is less than so_ext_len
The unsigned subtraction dl->length - so_ext_len wraps around to a huge value
dl->str + (huge value) points far out of bounds, and my_strcasecmp reads from that invalid address

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the exact bug fixed by the patch: when dl->length < so_ext_len, the unsigned subtraction underflows, causing an out-of-bounds memory access in my_strcasecmp (which internally calls my_strcasecmp_8bit). The CVE describes this as use-after-free/use-after-poison in my_strcasecmp_8bit, but the root cause is the same missing length check that the fix adds.

actual_issue_summary: When dl->length is smaller than so_ext_len, the unsigned subtraction `dl->length - so_ext_len` wraps around, causing `dl->str + dl->length - so_ext_len` to point to invalid memory. The fix adds a `dl->length < so_ext_len` guard before the subtraction to prevent this out-of-bounds access in my_strcasecmp_8bit.

cve_desc: A buffer overflow in the GmfOpenMesh() function of libMeshb v7.61 allows attackers to cause a Denial of Service (DoS) via a crafted MESH file.

finding.description: When reading an ASCII mesh file, fscanf with "%s" is used to scan tokens into a fixed-size stack buffer `str[GmfStrSiz]` without any width limit. A maliciously crafted mesh file containing a token longer than GmfStrSiz characters (with no whitespace) will cause a stack buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: GmfOpenMesh is called with a crafted .mesh file for reading.
The file is detected as ASCII mesh (.mesh extension), and the ASCII reading branch is entered.
fscanf(msh->hdl, "%s", str) is called in a loop to find "MeshVersionFormatted". The "%s" format has no width limit.

state.value: buffer of size GmfStrSiz on stack

step_by_step_execution.description: A crafted file contains a token longer than GmfStrSiz bytes with no whitespace, causing fscanf to write beyond the bounds of str.

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches the CVE. The fix changed "%s" to "%100s" in both fscanf calls to limit the scan width, preventing the buffer overflow in the stack-allocated str buffer.

actual_issue_summary: Two fscanf calls in the ASCII mesh reading path used "%s" without a field width limit when scanning into the fixed-size buffer str[GmfStrSiz]. A crafted .mesh file with an overly long token could overflow this buffer. The fix adds a width specifier ("%100s") to both fscanf calls.

cve_desc: Integer signedness error in the simplestring_addn function in simplestring.c in xmlrpc-epi through 0.54.2, as used in PHP before 5.5.38, 5.6.x before 5.6.24, and 7.x before 7.0.9, allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (heap-based buffer overflow) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a long first argument to the PHP xmlrpc_encode_request function.

finding.description: If add_len is negative (the parameter is a signed int), the size check `target->len + add_len + 1 > target->size` will likely evaluate to false, skipping the realloc. Then memcpy is called with a negative add_len which is implicitly converted to a very large size_t, causing a massive heap buffer overflow (read from source and write into target->str).

step_by_step_execution.description: add_len is a signed int parameter that receives a negative value due to a very long input string causing integer overflow

state.value: negative int (e.g. from large string length overflowing int)

step_by_step_execution.description: Size check target->len + add_len + 1 > target->size evaluates to false because adding a negative add_len reduces the sum

state.value: small value due to negative add_len

step_by_step_execution.description: memcpy is called with add_len implicitly converted to size_t, becoming a very large value
Heap buffer overflow occurs as memcpy writes far beyond the allocated buffer

judgments.reasoning: This directly describes the CVE: the integer signedness error where add_len is declared as signed int, allowing negative values that bypass the size check and cause heap buffer overflow via memcpy. The fix changes add_len to size_t.

finding.description: When target->len and add_len are both large positive ints, their sum can overflow (signed integer overflow is UB, but practically wraps). This can cause the size check to be bypassed or cause a too-small allocation via realloc, followed by a memcpy that writes far beyond the allocated buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: target->len and add_len are both large positive ints
Their sum overflows, causing incorrect size calculation
Realloc allocates too small a buffer or size check is bypassed
memcpy writes beyond allocated buffer

judgments.reasoning: This is another facet of the same CVE. The fix adds overflow checks (SIZE_MAX checks and post-calculation overflow check) and changes types to size_t, addressing both the signedness issue and the integer overflow in size calculations. Both findings describe components of the same vulnerability.

actual_issue_summary: Integer signedness error in simplestring_addn: add_len was declared as signed int, allowing a very long input to cause negative values or integer overflow in size calculations, bypassing the buffer size check and leading to heap buffer overflow via memcpy. The fix changes add_len and related variables to size_t and adds explicit overflow checks.

cve_desc: libmobi is vulnerable to Out-of-bounds Write

finding.description: If `offset` equals `INT_MIN`, `abs(INT_MIN)` is undefined behavior in C because the result cannot be represented as an `int`. This can lead to `aoffset` being an unexpected value (e.g., INT_MIN cast to size_t becomes a very large number), potentially bypassing subsequent bounds checks.

step_by_step_execution.description: offset is INT_MIN, abs(INT_MIN) is undefined behavior

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE (CWE-787, out-of-bounds write) is caused by a missing bounds check in the negative-offset branch: when offset is negative, the code did not verify that `buf->offset + len <= buf->maxlen`, allowing the memmove destination to write beyond the buffer. The fix adds `(buf->offset + len > buf->maxlen)` to the condition. Finding 0 describes a different theoretical issue (abs(INT_MIN) undefined behavior) that is not what the CVE or the fix addresses.

actual_issue_summary: When offset is negative, the function only checked that the source pointer didn't go before the buffer start, but failed to check that the destination write (buf->offset + len) didn't exceed buf->maxlen. This allowed an out-of-bounds write via memmove. The fix adds the missing check `buf->offset + len > buf->maxlen` in the negative offset branch.

cve_desc: A buffer overflow vulnerability in jetp3852_print_page() in devices/gdev3852.c of Artifex Software GhostScript v9.50 allows a remote attacker to escalate privileges via a crafted PDF file. This is fixed in v9.51.

finding.description: Stack buffer overflow via memset past the end of the `data` array. When `end_data` is close to `data + line_size` (e.g., the last bytes of the scan line are non-zero), writing 7 bytes at `end_data` can exceed the bounds of the `data[DATA_SIZE]` buffer. The check only ensures `line_size <= DATA_SIZE`, but `end_data` can be as high as `data + DATA_SIZE`, so the memset writes up to 7 bytes beyond the stack buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: line_size is computed and checked to be <= DATA_SIZE
end_data is set to data + line_size
Trailing zeros are trimmed, but if last byte is non-zero, end_data stays at data + line_size
memset writes 7 bytes starting at end_data, which can be up to 7 bytes past data[DATA_SIZE]

judgments.reasoning: The finding identifies a real buffer overflow that was part of the CVE fix. The vulnerable `memset(end_data, 0, 7)` was removed in the fix commit. When `end_data` equals `data + DATA_SIZE`, the memset writes 7 bytes beyond the stack-allocated `data` buffer. While the primary CVE issue also involves uninitialized data used as array indices (dp[] values > 7 indexing into size-8 lookup tables), the memset overflow is a genuine buffer overflow component that was fixed by the same commit. Both issues are buffer overflows in jetp3852_print_page() matching CVE-2020-16302.

actual_issue_summary: The `data` buffer was not fully initialized, so when `line_size < DATA_SIZE`, bytes beyond `line_size` in the buffer contained uninitialized values. The transpose loop iterates over the full `DATA_SIZE` and uses `dp[]` byte values as indices into lookup arrays of size 8 (spr40, spr8, spr2). Uninitialized bytes with values > 7 caused out-of-bounds array reads. The fix initializes the entire `data` buffer to zero and removes the insufficient `memset(end_data, 0, 7)` padding which could also write past the buffer boundary.

cve_desc: This affects the package juce-framework/JUCE before 6.1.5. This vulnerability is triggered when a malicious archive is crafted with an entry containing a symbolic link. When extracted, the symbolic link is followed outside of the target dir allowing writing arbitrary files on the target host. In some cases, this can allow an attacker to execute arbitrary code. The vulnerable code is in the ZipFile::uncompressEntry function in juce_ZipFile.cpp and is executed when the archive is extracted upon calling uncompressTo() on a ZipFile object.

finding.description: The entry filename from the zip archive is used directly with getChildFile without sanitizing path traversal sequences like '../'. A malicious zip file can contain entries with filenames such as '../../etc/crontab' which would cause files to be written outside the intended target directory (Zip Slip attack).

step_by_step_execution.description: The entry filename is read from the zip archive entry.
targetFile is computed by joining the target directory with the attacker-controlled entry path, resolving '..' sequences.
No check is performed to verify targetFile is within targetDirectory.
A FileOutputStream is opened and data is written to the attacker-chosen path outside the target directory.

judgments.reasoning: The CVE describes writing arbitrary files outside the target directory during zip extraction. The fix adds an isAChildOf check to prevent path traversal. This finding correctly identifies that missing check, describing the Zip Slip / path traversal component of CVE-2021-23521. While the CVE description emphasizes symbolic links, the fix addresses both symlink following AND path traversal (the isAChildOf check), and this finding captures the path traversal aspect.

actual_issue_summary: CVE-2021-23521 involves two related issues in ZipFile::uncompressEntry: (1) no validation that the resolved target file path stays within the target directory (path traversal / Zip Slip), and (2) no check for symbolic links in parent directories that could redirect writes outside the target directory. The fix adds an isAChildOf check to prevent path traversal and a hasSymbolicPart check to prevent symlink-based escapes.
